Facts.text: Saint Boniface IV begins his reign as Catholic Pope
Eastern Roman Emperor Constans II is assassinated in his bath at Syracuse, Italy
Saint Ludmila is murdered at the command of her daughter-in-law at Tetin
Thomas Wolsey appointed English Archbishop of York
San Lorenzo del Escorial Palace in Madrid finished
Giambattista Catagna elected as Pope Urban VII
Battle of Sekigahara, rise of the Tokugawa shogunate in Japan
First non-aristocratic, free public school in Europe is opened in Frascati, Italy
Prince Bethlen Gabor's troops occupy Pozsony (Pressburg) Hungary
Swedish troops occupy Riga
The Peach War: Munsee attack Dutch colony of New Amsterdam, killing about 40 colonists, possibly in retaliation for the murder of a Munsee woman picking peaches
England & France sign peace treaty
Germantown in Pennsylvania is founded by 13 immigrant families
Ferenc Rákóczi II, Prince of Transylvania and TsarPeter the Greatsign social security agreement
King Frederik Willem I divides Prussia-Brandenburg in Cantons
Battle of Signal Hill, the last battle of the North American theatre of the Seven Years' War
Cossack pretender to the Russian throne Yemelyan Ivanovich Pugachev captured
British forces capture Kip's Bay, Manhattan, during Revolution
-16] Utrecht patriots flee to Amsterdam
US Department of Foreign Affairs, renamed Department of State
Dutch East India controlled Cape Colony (present day South Africa) surrenders to Britain
Constitutionalist revolution in Lisbon, Portugal
Act of Independence of Central America: Costa Rica, El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras and Nicaragua declare their independence from the Spanish Empire
First National Negro Convention begins in Philadelphia
The locomotive "John Bull" operates for the first time in New Jersey on the Camden and Amboy Railroad
Jung Bahadur Rana grabs power in Nepal
Saint Joseph's University is founded in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania
Antoinette Blackwell is the first US woman to be ordained a minister
Timothy Alder of NY patents a typesetting machine
Confederates conquer Union-weapon arsenal at Harpers Ferry, West Virginia
Dutch 1st Chamber abolishes Capital punishment (20-18)
Last German troops leave France
Pim Mulier forms Royal Haarlemsche Football Club based in Haarlem, Netherlands; oldest existing club in Dutch football
British General Wolseley occupies Cairo
Philadelphia celebrates 100th anniversary of US Constitution
"Svoboda", Ukrainian language weekly newspaper founded by Father Hryhorii Hrushka, in Jersey City, New Jersey; expands to daily in 1921, reverts to weekly in 1998
Japan defeats China in Battle of Ping Yang
National Afro-American Council forms in Rochester NY
A Boer delegation issues an appeal at the Hague, Netherlands, that the major powers intervene in the war in South Africa
Chicago Cubs infielders Tinker, Evers, & Chance turn their first double play together in a 6-3 win over Cincinnati
Boers & Afrikaners win 1st general elections in Union of South-Africa
Boston Red Sox pitcher "Smoky" Joe Wood ties then MLB record of 16 straight wins with a 2-1 victory over St. Louis Browns at Sportsman's Park
War between Turkey & Montenegro breaks out in Albania
1st US milch goat show held, Rochester, NY
First Battle of Aisne finishes, Germans vs. French & British during WWI
US Marines march out of Vera Cruz, Mexico
WWI: Battle of Aisne ends with Germans beating French forces in France
Forbes Magazine founded by B. C. Forbes and Walter Drey in the US
Russia proclaimed a republic by Alexander Kerensky's Provisional government
WBZ-AM in Boston MA begins radio transmissions
Philadelphia catcher Butch Henline becomes first NLer to hit 3 HRs in a game since 1897 during Phillies' 10-9 win over St. Louis Cardinals at the Baker Bowl
Governor Walton of Oklahoma declares state of siege because of Ku Klux Klan terror
400 kg Fournier-albums (forged postage stamps) burn in Geneva
St. Louis Cardinals set NL record of 18 men left-on-base; still beat Phillies, 8-6 at the Baker Bowl, Philadelphia
Stothard, Kalmar & Ruby's musical "Good Boy" premieres in NYC
1st international bridge match is held in London. US team defeats England
British naval fleet mutinies at Invergordon over pay cuts
Philadelphia A's clinch pennant, beating Cleveland
Nuremberg Laws deprives German Jews of citizenship & makes swastika official symbol of Nazi Germany
Works Progress Administration (WPA) extends L-Taraval streetcar line to San Francisco Zoo, at Sloat Blvd (San Francisco, California)
John Cobb sets world auto speed record at 350.2 MPH (lasts 1 day)
MLB Pittsburgh Pirates outfielders Llod Waner and PAul Waner become first and only brothers hit back-to-back homeruns
3rd American Football League plays 1st game (Milw 14, Columbus 2)
Chicago Tribune sponsors Ted Lyons Day (White Sox pitcher)
Nazis kill 800 Jewish women at Shkudvil, Lithuania
US aircraft carrier Wasp torpedoed at Guadalcanal
Concentration Camp Kauwen in Lithuania opens
Concentration Camp Vaivara in Estonia opens
British bombers hit German battleship Tirpitz with Tallboy bombs
Soviet troops free Sofia, Bulgaria
US 1st Infantry division pushes through to Westwall
US 28th Infantry division occupies Hill 555 at Roscheid
US troops land on islands of Palau and Morotai, western Pacific
A hurricane in southern Florida and the Bahamas destroys 366 planes and 25 blimps at NAS Richmond
Dodgers beat Cubs 2-0 in 5 inns, games called because of gnats
First four-engined jet-propelled fighter plane tested, Columbus, Ohio
RCA releases the 12AX7 miniature dual triode vacuum tube; it is still in production
Typhoon Kathleen hit Tone River, Saitama and Tokyo area, killing at least 1,930 and injuring 1,750
Yanks clinch pennant #15
F-86 Sabre sets world aircraft speed record of 1080 kph
WHN-AM in NY City changes call letters to WMGM
"Lone Ranger" premieres on ABC-TV. Hi-yo, Silver! Away!
WJAC TV channel 6 in Johnstown, PA (NBC/ABC) begins broadcasting
WJXT TV channel 4 in Jacksonville, FL (CBS) begins broadcasting
During Korean conflict, UN forces land at Inchon in South Korea
East German premier Grotewohl pleads for German reunification
For a record 6th time, NY Yankees' first baseman Johnny Mize hits 3 HRs in one game
Longest game in Phila's Shribe Park, Phils beat Reds 8-7 in 19
US troop land on Wolmi-Do island off of Seoul
Emile Zatopek runs world record 20k (1:01:15.8)
UN turns over Eritrea to Ethiopia
Boxing's NBA adopts 10-pt-must-scoring-system (10 pts to round winner)
KVOA TV channel 4 in Tucson, AZ (NBC) begins broadcasting
WVEC TV channel 13 in Hampton-Norfolk, VA (ABC) begins broadcasting
WCTV TV channel 6 in Tallahassee-Thomasville, Florida (CBS) begins
SF Seals (Pacific Coast League) play their last game
Commuter train crashes off an open drawbridge, killing 48 in Bayonne, New Jersey
Australia's 1st entry in America's Cup yacht race (US wins)
USSR performs nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya USSR
WOKR TV channel 13 in Rochester, NY (ABC) begins broadcasting
Alou brothers - Felipe, Matty, & Jesús - appear in San Francisco Giants outfield for 1 inning in a 13-5 win over Pittsburgh Pirates at Forbes Field in Pittsburgh
Church bombing in Birmingham Alabama, kills 4 African-American girls
WNTV TV channel 29 in Greenville, SC (PBS) begins broadcasting
Final edition of socialist British newspaper "Daily Herald"

The Beatles play at Public Auditorium in Cleveland, Ohio; police stop show during third song as some of the crowd climbed on the stage, after a 10 minute delay, and warning audience to stay seated, the concert resumed with the.
US TV series "Lost in Space" premieres
Dutch political party (D'66) forms
First British nuclear ballistic missile submarine HMS Resolution launched
Gemini XI (Conrad/Gordon) returns to Earth
KPOB TV channel 15 in Poplar Bluff, MO (ABC) begins broadcasting
Launch of Zond 5, 1st lunar fly-around with Earth reentry
NY Zendo (Shoboji) was opened by S Nakagawa & D S Harada
Probable Test flight for a manned fly-around (scooped by Apollo 8)
WUAB TV channel 43 in Lorain-Cleveland, Ohio (IND) begins broadcasting
WXON TV channel 20 in Detroit, MI (IND) begins broadcasting
Officers of the Royal Ulster Constabulary (RUC) in Northern Ireland vote narrowly in favour of remaining unarmed
PLO leader Arafat threatens to make a cemetery of Jordan
Rotterdam dock strikes end
A magnitude 4.5 earthquake shakes Northern Illinois.
An SAS domestic flight from Gothenburg to Stockholm was hijacked and flown to Malmö-Bulltofta Airport.
WMAO TV channel 23 in Greenwood, MS (PBS) begins broadcasting
Dutch Guilder devalued by 5%
Ohio State's Archie Griffith begins record 31 cons 100 yd rushing
OPEC supports price hikes and designates six Gulf countries to negotiate collectively with companies over prices; other members to negotiate individually
Air Vietnam flight 727 is hijacked, then crashes while attempting to land with 75 on board.
Market Square Arena in Indianapolis opens
Mike Vail extends hitting streak ton rookie-record 23 straight game
The French département of Corse (the entire island of Corsica) is divided into two: Haute-Corse and Corse-du-Sud.
Ntozake Shange's play "For Colored Girls Who Have Considered Suicide / When the Rainbow Is Enuf" premieres in NYC
Soyuz 22 carries 2 cosmonauts into Earth orbit for 8 days
Orioles forfeit to Blue Jays when mgrEarl Weaverpulls team off field in 5th citing hazardous condition (small tarpaulin on bullpen mound)
TV LA drama "CHiPs" debuts on NBC in US
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Dodgers become 1st major league team to draw 3 million fans
Red Sox Bob Watson is 1st to hit for cycle in AL & NL (Astros)
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
The John Bull becomes the oldest operable steam locomotive in the world when the Smithsonian Institution operates it under its own power outside Washington, D.C.
Vanuatu becomes a member of the United Nations.
First issue of "USA Today" published by Gannett Co Inc
Israeli forces began pouring into west Beirut
PopeJohn Paul IIreceives PLO leader Yasser Arafat
Frances Schreuder is convicted for the first-degree murder of Franklin Bradshaw
Police officers beat Michael Stewart to death for graffiting NYC subway
The first Costco store opens in Seattle, Washington
Morocco Showcase opens
USSR performs nuclear test at Eastern Kazakh, Semipalitinsk USSR
MLB New York Yankees trade 25-year-old pitching prospect Jim Deshaies to Houston Astros for 40-year-old pitcher Joe Niekro
1st broadcast of legal drama "L.A. Law" created by Steven Bochco, with ensemble cast including Corbin Bersen, Jill Eikenberry and Harry Hamlin on NBC
Bomb attack in Paris, 1 dead
Italy sends a naval contingent to the Persian Gulf
PopeJohn Paul IIarrives in Los Angeles for a two-day papal visit, and addresses U.S. entertainment leaders
US Senate Judiciary Committee begins confirmation hearings for Supreme Court nominee Robert Bork
Lillehammer, Norway, upsets Anchorage to host 1994 Winter olympics
Museum of Moving Image in London opens
Test Cricket debut of Ian Healy, vs Pakistan at Karachi
The U.S. Congress recognizes American journalist Terry Anderson's continued captivity in Beirut, Lebanon
Chicago White Sox Bobby Thigpen is 1st to record 50 saves
Florida lottery goes over $100,000,000
Charles Vand Maria of Hungary return to Spain and retire to the Monastery of Yuste in Extremadura
Giambattista Pamfili replaces Pope Urban VIII asInnocent X
George Washington, on authorization of Congress, appointsCasimir Pulaskias brigadier general in Continental Army cavalry
"Lyrical Ballads" published bySamuel Taylor ColeridgeandWilliam Wordsworth- 1st work of English Romantic movement (probable date)
Duke of WellingtonopensGeorge Stephenson's Liverpool and Manchester Railway
William Huskissonbecomes the first passenger killed by a railway train while attending the opening of the Liverpool and Manchester Railway at Parkside station in England
HMS Beagle, withCharles Darwinon board, reaches the Galápagos Islands
Queen Wilhelminacalls Dutch railroad strikers "criminals"
PopeBenedict XVpublishes encyclical Alcohol Paraclitus
Military officerMiguel Primo de Riverabecomes dictator and Prime Minister of Spain after launching a coup d'état
Scottish bacteriologistAlexander Flemingdiscovers penicillin while sorting through petri dishes containing colonies of the bacteria Staphylococcus
British PMNeville ChamberlainvisitsAdolf Hitlerat Berchtesgaden, Germany
Jazz piano prodigy Margaret Johnson (20) makes her only recording, four sides withBillie Holidayin NYC: Johnson dies of tuberculosis less than a year later
UK Prime MinisterWinston Churchillvisits Royal Air Force 11th Fighter Group on what would be the fiercest day of the Battle of Britain
Benito Mussoliniforms a rival anti-monarchist fascist government in Italy and soon returns to power as a German puppet state
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Ingruentium Malorum
Konrad Adenauer's CDU wins parliamentary election in West Germany
US TV series "Bachelor Father" withJohn Forsythepremieres
Soviet PremierNikita Khrushchevarrives in United States to begin a 13-day visit, the first state visit of a Soviet or Russian leader to the US
Maurice Richardannounces his retirement. He finishes his career with 544 goals, an NHL record at the time.
Ahmed Ben Bellais elected as first president of the newly independent Algeria
The Beatles headline The Great Prop Prom at Royal Albert Hall, London;The Rolling Stonesare one of the opening acts
Volt/Stax records releasesOtis Redding's third studio album "Otis Blue - Otis Redding Sings Soul" in the US, Atlantic releases it in the UK; frequently ranked on top album lists
U.S. PresidentLyndon B. Johnson, responding to a sniper attack at the University of Texas at Austin, writes a letter to the United States Congress urging the enactment of gun control legislation.
"Barbra Streisand: A Happening in Central Park" premieres on CBS TV
MLB St. Louis CardinalsSteve Carltonsets record by striking out 19 NY Mets in a game
Decca awardsBing Crosbya second platinum disc for selling 300 million records
First broadcast of "Columbo" starringPeter Falkon NBC
Apple Records releasesJohn LennonandYoko Ono's political album "Sometime in NYC" in UK, delayed by publishing rights dispute; includes some live tracks from 1971 concert withFrank Zappa
Carl XVI Gustafascends the throne, becoming King of Sweden (1973-present)
Secretariatwins Marlboro Cup in world record 1:45 2/5 for 1¼ miles
US PresidentJimmy Cartermeets with 15 record company executives
Muhammad AlibeatsLeon Spinksin 15 rounds for heavyweight boxing title
Paul McCartneyreleases single "Temporary Secretary"
PopeJohn Paul IIpublishes encyclical "Laborem exercens" against capitalism/Marxism
US Senate Judiciary Committee unanimously approvesSandra Day O'Connorfor the US Supreme Court
Israel premierMenachem Beginresigns
CBS premieres its TV adaptation ofArthur Miller's "Death of a Salesman", starringDustin Hoffman,John Malkovich, Kate Reid, Stephen Lang, and Charles Durning
Olof Palmeforms Sweden minority government
Napoleon Bonaparteand his French army reach the Kremlin in Moscow, where they watch the flames of the Great Fire of Moscow spread and grow
First use of tanks in warfare, Britain's Mark I "Little Willies" at the Battle of Flers-Courcelette, part of the Battle of the Somme
Battle of Britain Day: Royal Air Force repulses a major Luftwaffe attack, losing 29 aircraft to the Germans' 57-61 as the tide begins to turn
Maria, Queen of Hungary as wife of Louis II and Governor of the Habsburg Netherlands (1531-55), born in Brussels (d. 1558)
Catherine of Austria, Queen of Poland and Grand Duchess consort of Lithuania, born in Innsbruck or Vienna, Austria (d. 1572)
Elisabeth of Anhalt-Zerbst, Electress of Brandenburg, by marriage, born in Zerbst, Electorate of Saxony (d. 1607)
Erasmus Widmann, German composer, born in Free Imperial City of Schwäbisch Hall, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1634)
Charles Annibal Fabrot, French lawyer, born in Aix-en-Provence, France (d. 1659)
Cristóbal de Isla Diego, Spanish Baroque composer and conductor, baptized in Berlanga de Duero, Spain (d. 1651)
François VI, duc de La Rochefoucauld, French writer of maxims and memoirs, born in Paris, Kingdom of France (d. 1680)
Titus Oates, English minister and plotter, born in Oakham, Rutland, England (d. 1705)
Princess Sophia Dorothea of Celle, repudiated wife of future King George I of Great Britain, and mother of George II, born in Celle, Germany (d. 1726)
Gottfried Kirchhoff, German organist and composer, chiefly of sacred music, born in Mühlbeck, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1746)
Ignazio Prota, Italian composer, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples, Spanish Empire (d. 1748)

Jean-Sylvain Bailly, French astronomer noted for his computation of an orbit for Halley’s Comet (1759) and author of History of Ancient Astronomy from Its Origin to the Establishment of the School of Alexandria, born in Paris (d. 1793)
Georg Ritschel, German composer, born in Mannheim, Electorate of the Palatinate, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1805)
Bogislav Friedrich Emanuel von Tauentzien, Prussian general of the Napoleonic Wars, born in Potsdam, Brandenburg (d. 1824)
Friedrich Heine, German composer, born in Leipzig, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1821)
Paolo Francesco Parenti, Italian composer, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1821)
Louis Clapisson, French composer and violinist, born in Naples, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1866)
Jan Nepomuk Škroup, Czech composer, born in Osice, Kingdom of Bohemia, Austrian Empire (d. 1892)
Willem Josephus van Zeggelen, Dutch author, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1879)
Edward Wolff, Polish pianist and composer, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1880)
Aleksandr Mikhailovich Butlerov, Russian chemist who discovered hexamine in 1859, born in Chistopol, Kazan Governorate, Russian Empire (d. 1886)
Heinrich von Treitschke, German historian and political writer, born in Dresden, Kingdom of Saxony (d. 1896)
Leo van Gheluwe, Belgian composer, born in Wannegem-Lede, East Flanders (d. 1914)
Maurice, Prince of the Netherlands, son of King William III, born in Noordeinde Palace, The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1850)
Josif Marinković, Serbian composer, and choral director, born in Vranjevo, Novi Bečej, Austrian Empire (d. 1931)
Edward Bouchet, American physicist, 1st African American to receive US Ph.D, born in New Haven, Connecticut (d. 1918)
Jan Matzeliger, Surinamese-Dutch-American inventor (shoe lasting machine), born in Paramaribo, Dutch Guyana (now Suriname) (d. 1889)
Charles Eugène, vicomte de Foucauld, French explorer and a hermit, born in Strasbourg, French Empire (d. 1916)
Jenő Hubay [Huber], Hungarian violinist, composer, and educator (Budapest Conservatory, 1886-1934), born in Pest, Hungary (d. 1937)
M. Visvesvaraya, Indian civil engineer and statesman (19th Diwan of the Mysore Kingdom), born in Muddenahalli, Chikkaballapura, Kingdom of Mysore, born in (d. 1962)
Horatio Parker, American organist and composer, born in Auburndale, Massachusetts (d. 1919)
Henri Capitant, French lawyer (Loi Falcidie), born in Grenoble, France (d. 1937)
Nicolaus Adriani, Dutch linguist (Middle-Celebes languages), born in Oud-Loosdrecht, North Holland, Netherlands (d. 1926)
Dirk Berend Nanninga, Dutch painter, born in Arnhem, Netherlands (d. 1954)
Bruno Walter, German-French opera and symphony conductor (Vienna Philharmonic; NY Philharmonic), born in Berlin, Kingdom of Prussia, German Empire (d. 1962)
Frank Gannett, American newspaper publisher (Gannett), born in Rochester, New York (d. 1957)
Sarat Chandra Chattopadhyay, Indian Bengali novelist (Devdas), born in Debanandapur, Hooghly district, Bengal Presidency, India (d. 1938)
Jakob Ehrlich, Austrian politician and Zionist, born in Bystřice pod Hostýnem, Czech Republic (d. 1938)
Max Factor, Polish-American make-up artist, inventor and founder of cosmetics manufacturer Max Factor & Company, born in Zduńska Wola, Poland, Russian Empire (d. 1938)
Alec Ross, Scottish golfer (US Open 1907), born in Dornoch, Scotland (d. 1952)
Joseph Lyons, 10th Prime Minister of Australia (1932-39), born in Stanley, Tasmania (d. 1939)
Esteban Terradas i Illa, Spanish mathematician, scientist and engineer, born in Barcelona (d. 1950)
Floro Ugarte, Argentine composer (From My Land; Saika), and educator, born in Buenos Aires, Argentina (d. 1975)
Carlos Dávila, President of Government Junta of Chile (1932), born in Los Ángeles, Chile (d. 1955)
Antonio Ascari, Italian auto racer (winner 4 x F1 Grand Prix), born in Bonferraro Di Sorgà, Italy (d. 1925)
Robert Benchley, American humorist (My 10 Years in a Quandary), born in Worcester, Massachusetts (d. 1945)
Claude McKaye, Jamaican-American author (Songs of Jamaica, Banjo), born in Clarendon Parish, Jamaica (d. 1948)
Frank Martin, Swiss composer (In Terra Fax), born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1974)
Silpa Bhirasri [Corrado Feroci], Italian-Thai sculptor, born in Florence, Italy (d. 1962)
Jean Renoir, French film director (Madame Bovary, Nana, Grand Illusion) and actor (Rules of the Game), born in Paris, France (d. 1979)
Oskar Klein, Swedish theoretical physicist known for Kaluza–Klein theory, born in Mörby, Sweden (d. 1977)
Magda Lupescu, Romanian mistress then consort of King Carol II of Romania, born in Iași, Romania (d. 1977) date of birth disputed
J. Slauerhoff, Dutch poet, writer and ship's doctor (El Dorado), born in Leeuwarden, Netherlands (d. 1936)
Milton S. Eisenhower, American University President (Pennsylvania State) and brother of Dwight, born in Abilene, Kansas (d. 1985)
Donald Bailey, British civil engineer, invented the Bailey bridge, born in Rotherham, England (d. 1985)
Roy Acuff, American country fiddler, singer ("Wabash Cannonball"; Grand Ole Opry), and music publisher (Acuff-Rose Music), born in Maynardville, Tennessee (d. 1992)
Syed Wazir Ali, Indian cricket batsman (7 Tests; Southern Punjab; Muslims), born in Jalandhar, India (d. 1950)
Philip Lee, Australian cricket all-rounder (2 Tests, 5 wickets; South Australia), born in Gladstone, Australia (d. 1980)
Tom Conway, Russian actor (Mark Saber, Betty Hutton Show), born in St Petersburg, Russian Empire (d. 1967)
Umberto II, of Italy, the last King of Italy (ruled for 24 days in 1946), born in the Castle of Racconigi in Piedmont, Italy (d. 1983)
Kathryn Murray (née Kohnfelder), American dancer (Arthur Murray Dance Party), born in Jersey City, New Jersey (d. 1999)
Jack Bailey, American TV host (Queen for a Day), born in Hampton, Iowa (d. 1980)
Gerd Gaiser, German writer (Ship in the Mountain), born in Oberriexingen, Germany (d. 1976)
Penny Singleton, American actress (Blondie) and voice actress (Jane-Jetsons), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2003)
C. N. Annadurai, Indian politician and 1st Chief Minister of Tamil Nadu, born in Kancheepuram, Madras, British India (d. 1969)
Carlos Estrada, Uruguayan composer and conductor, born in Montevideo, Uruguay (d. 1970)
Phil Arnold, American actor (Errand Boy, Stud Lonigan, Damn Yankees), born in Hackensack, New Jersey (d. 1968)
George Kilpatrick, Canadian-British bible scholar (University of Oxford), born in Coal Creek, British Columbia (d. 1989)
Richard Baerlein, British horse racing journalist (Evening Standard 1947-57, Observer 1963-95, Guardian 1968-95), and author, born in England (d. 1995)
Karsten Solheim, American golf entrepreneur (PING, Solheim Cup), born in Bergen, Norway (d. 2000)
Luther Terry, 9th Surgeon General of the United States (1961-65) warned against smoking, born in Red Level, Alabama (d. 1985)
Silas Hogan, American blues singer and guitarist, born in Westover, Louisiana (d. 1994)
Gisela Hernández [Gonzalo], Cuban composer (Diálogo), and music educator, born in Cardenas, Cuba (d. 1971)
Ismail Yasseen, Egyptian comedian and actor (Isamil Yassine as Tarzan, Ibn Hamidu), born in Suez, Egypt (d. 1972)
Hans Filbinger, German politician (CDU), born in Mannheim, German Empire (d. 2007)
Henry Brant, American composer (Great American Goot), born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2008)
John Mitchell, American lawyer, political operative (US Attorney General, 1969-72), and convicted perjurer (Watergate Scandal), born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1988)
Roger "Ram" Ramirez, American jazz pianist and composer, born in San Juan, Puerto Rico (d. 1994)
Adolfo Bioy Casares, Argentine writer known for "The Invention of Morel" and collaborator with Borges, born in Buenos Aires (d. 1999)
Creighton Abrams, American general (commander of American forces in the Vietnam War, 1968-72), born in Springfield, Massachusetts (d. 1974)
Jens Otto Krag, Danish politician, premier of Denmark (1962-68, 71-72), born in Randers, Denmark (d. 1978)
Helmut Schoen, football coach (manager of West Germany (1964-1978, 1974 World Cup), born in Dresden, German Empire (d. 1996)
John Conte, American actor (The Man with the Golden Arm, Lost in a Harem, Mantovani), born in Palmer, Massachusetts (d. 2006)
Margaret Lockwood, British actress (Cast a Dark Shadow, The Lady Vanishes), born in Karachi, India (d. 1990)
Richard Arnell, English classical composer, born in London, England (d. 2009)
Nipsey Russell, American comedian (Car 54, Where Are You?), born in Atlanta, Georgia (d. 2005)
Nelson Gidding, American screenwriter (The Andromeda Strain), born in New York (d. 2004)
Dave Garcia, American baseball manager (California Angels, Cleveland Indians), born in San Diego, California (d. 2018)
Jan Frank Fischer, Czech composer, born in Louny, Czech Republic (d. 2006)
Norma MacMillan, Canadian voice actress (Casper the Ghost), born in Vancouver (d. 2001)
Bob Anderson, English fencer and renowned film fight choreographer (Star Wars, Lord of the Rings), born in Gosport, England (d. 2012)
Jackie Cooper, American actor and director (Skippy, Superman), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2011)
Anton Heiller, Austrian organist and composer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1979)
Arvell Shaw, American jazz double-bassist (Louis Armstrong; Teddy Wilson), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 2002)
David Kraehenbuehl, American composer (March of the Trolls), pianist, teacher (Yale), and music theorist (Journal of Music Theory), born in Champaign-Urbana, Illinois (d. 1997)
Bobby Short, American cabaret singer and pianist (Carlisle Hotel), born in Danville, Illinois (d. 2005)
Lucebert [Jacobus Swaanswijk], Dutch poet, painter and cartoonist (Boozz, PC Hooft prize 1967), born in Amsterdam, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Carlo Rambaldi, Italian special effects artist (Alien, E.T.), born in Vigarano Mainarda, Italy (d. 2012)
Forrest Compton, American actor (Gomer Pyle USMC, Edge of Night), born in Reading, Pennsylvania (d. 2020)
Gösta Jonsson, Swedish saxophonist, accordion player and bandleader, born in Kungsholmen, Stockholm, Sweden (d. 1984)
Wally Halder, Canadian ice hockey forward (Olympic gold 1948, tournament top scorer), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1994)
Edward Derwinski, American politician (1st Secretary of Veteran Affairs 1989-92; US Representative from Illinois, 1959-83), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2012)
Jean-Pierre Serre, French mathematician (Fields Medal, 1954; Wolf Prize, 2000; Abel Prize, 2003), born in Bages, France
Shohei Imamura, Japanese film director, won 2 Palme d'Or awards for "The Ballad of Narayama" and "The Eel", born in Tokyo, Japan (d. 2006)
David Stove, Australian philosopher who held a competition to find the "worst argument in the world", born in Moree, New South Wales, (d. 1994)
Norm Crosby, American comedian and "Master of the Malaprop" (Liar's Club), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 2020)
Julian "Cannonball" Adderley, American jazz alto saxophonist and bandleader of the 1950-60s hard bop era ("The Black Messiah"), born in Tampa, Florida (d. 1975)
Rod Robbie, Canadian architect and planner (Roger's Centre/Sky Dome, Toronto), born in Poole, England (d. 2012)
Eva Burrows, Austrian Salvation Army officer, 13th General of The Salvation Army, born in Newcastle, Australia (d. 2015)
Murray Gell-Mann, American physicist who predicted quarks (1969 Nobel Prize for Physics for theory of elementary particles), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 2019)
Patsy O. Sherman, American chemist and co-inventor of Scotchgard, born in Minneapolis, Minnesota (d. 2008)[1]
Henry Darrow [Enrique Delgado], American stage and screen character actor (The High Chaparral; Harry O; The New Dick Van Dyke Show), born in New York City (d. 2021)
Rafael Frühbeck de Burgos, Spanish conductor, born in Burgos, Spain (d. 2014)
Fred Nile, Australian politician and minister, born in Sydney, Australia
Bill Pinkney, American National Sailing HOF yachtsman (first African American to sail around the world solo via Cape of Good Hope and Cape Horn), born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2023)
Edna Adan Ismail, Somali activist and the first qualified-nurse midwife in Somaliland, born in Hargeisa, British Somaliland
Fernando de la Rúa, Argentine politician, 51st President of Argentina (1999-2001), born in Argentina (d. 2019)
Jacques d'Ancona, Dutch journalist, theatre critic, and TV personality (Sound-Mix Show), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 2024)
Les Braid, British bassist (Swinging Blue Jeans - "Hippie-Hippie Shake"), born in West Derby, Liverpool, England (d. 2005)
Robert Lucas, Jr., American economist (Nobel Prize for Economics - 1995, for rational expectations theory), born in Yakima, Washington (d. 2023)[1]
Tony Yates, American college basketball point guard (National C'ship 1961, 62 Cincinnati Bearcats) and head coach (Cincinnati 1983-89), born in Lawrenceburg, Indiana (d. 2020)
Rafael Osuna, Mexican tennis player (US Nat C'ship 1963), born in Mexico City (d. 1969)
